name: React Advanced Patterns
description: Senior-level React — server components, concurrent features, advanced hooks, and performance deep dives.

cards.front: What are React Server Components (RSC) and why do they exist?
Server Components vs Client Components — what are the key differences and boundaries?
cards.back:
Server Components:
• Default in Next.js App Router
• Can be async, fetch data directly
• No hooks (useState, useEffect) — no interactivity
• Can import Client Components

Client Components:
• Marked with 'use client' directive at top of file
• Can use hooks, event handlers, browser APIs
• Cannot import Server Components (but can receive them as children/props)

Boundary rules:
• 'use client' creates a boundary — everything imported becomes client
• Pass Server Components as children to avoid pulling them client-side

```jsx
// ✅ Good: Server Component passed as child
<ClientWrapper>
  <ServerComponent />
</ClientWrapper>
```

cards.front: How does React Suspense work under the hood?
cards.back:
Suspense catches promises thrown during render and shows a fallback until they resolve.

Mechanism:
• A child throws a promise (e.g., lazy load, data fetch)
• Suspense catches it, renders fallback
• When promise resolves, React re-renders the subtree
• Multiple Suspense boundaries can nest for granular loading states

```jsx
<Suspense fallback={<Skeleton />}>
  <UserProfile />   {/* may suspend */}
  <Suspense fallback={<Spinner />}>
    <UserPosts />  {/* independent loading */}
  </Suspense>
</Suspense>
```

Key details:
• Works with React.lazy, RSC, and Suspense-enabled data fetching
• Streaming SSR sends fallback HTML first, swaps in content
• Avoid suspending at the root — too coarse

cards.front: What do useTransition and startTransition do? When would you use them?
What is useDeferredValue and when should you reach for it?
cards.back:
useDeferredValue returns a 'stale' copy of a value that lags behind during heavy renders.

```jsx
const deferredQuery = useDeferredValue(query);
// deferredQuery updates AFTER urgent renders finish

return (
  <>
    <input value={query} onChange={e => setQuery(e.target.value)} />
    <HeavyList filter={deferredQuery} />
  </>
);
```

useDeferredValue vs useTransition:
• useTransition — you control WHEN the update happens (wrap setState)
• useDeferredValue — you defer a VALUE you receive (e.g., from props)

Use useDeferredValue when:
• You don't control the state update (value comes from parent/URL)
• You want to show stale UI briefly while new UI renders
• Pair with memo() on the heavy child for best results

cards.front: How do React.lazy and dynamic imports work for code splitting?
cards.back:
React.lazy lets you lazy-load a component so its code is only fetched when first rendered.

```jsx
const Chart = React.lazy(() => import('./Chart'));

function Dashboard() {
  return (
    <Suspense fallback={<Spinner />}>
      <Chart data={data} />
    </Suspense>
  );
}
```

How it works:
• import() returns a promise for the module
• React.lazy wraps it so React can suspend on it
• Must be wrapped in Suspense for the fallback
• Webpack/Vite auto-split into a separate chunk

Best practices:
• Split at route level (biggest wins)
• Use webpackChunkName for readable chunk names
• Prefetch likely-needed chunks: import(/* webpackPrefetch: true */ './Chart')
• React.lazy only works for default exports

cards.front: Explain the compound component pattern in React.
cards.back:
Compound components share implicit state via context, giving users flexible composition.

```jsx
// API — clean, declarative
<Select value={val} onChange={setVal}>
  <Select.Trigger />
  <Select.Menu>
    <Select.Option value="a">Alpha</Select.Option>
    <Select.Option value="b">Beta</Select.Option>
  </Select.Menu>
</Select>
```

How to build:
• Parent creates context with shared state
• Child components consume that context
• Attach children as static properties (Select.Option)

Why use it:
• Users control layout and ordering
• No prop drilling — state is implicit
• Extensible without breaking changes

Examples in the wild:
• Radix UI, Headless UI, Reach UI
• HTML's own <select>/<option> is the inspiration

cards.front: Render props vs hooks — when does each pattern make sense?
cards.back:
Hooks replaced MOST render prop use cases, but render props still have a niche.

Use hooks when:
• You need to reuse stateful logic (90% of cases)
• Cleaner, less nesting, composable
```jsx
const { data } = useFetch('/api/users');
```

Use render props when:
• The parent needs to control WHAT renders based on child state
• You're building a headless library component
• You need to avoid re-mounting on logic change
```jsx
<Virtualized items={list}>
  {({ item, style }) => <Row style={style}>{item.name}</Row>}
</Virtualized>
```

Still common in:
• Downshift, React Spring (older API), react-window
• Animation libraries where child rendering varies

Rule of thumb: hooks first, render props when the CONSUMER decides the JSX.

cards.front: What are React context performance pitfalls and how do you fix them?
cards.back:
Pitfall: ANY context value change re-renders ALL consumers — even if they only use part of it.

Common mistakes:
• Putting unrelated state in one context
• Creating new object references every render
```jsx
// ❌ New object every render
<Ctx.Provider value={{ user, theme }}>
```

Fixes:
• Split contexts — separate UserCtx from ThemeCtx
• Memoize the value object:
```jsx
const value = useMemo(() => ({ user, theme }), [user, theme]);
<Ctx.Provider value={value}>
```
• Use state + dispatch pattern (like useReducer) — dispatch is stable
• Use selector libraries: use-context-selector or Zustand
• React 19's use() + memo can help avoid unnecessary re-renders

Rule: If context changes often, consider moving to external state (Zustand, Jotai).

cards.front: What are forwardRef and useImperativeHandle? When do you need them?
What are best practices for writing custom hooks?
How do React error boundaries work? What are their limitations?
What are React Portals and when should you use them?
cards.back:
Portals render children into a DOM node OUTSIDE the parent component's DOM hierarchy. ```jsx import { createPortal } from 'react-dom'; function Modal({ children }) { return createPortal(
{children}
, document.getElementById('modal-root') ); } ``` Why portals exist: • Escape CSS overflow: hidden, z-index stacking issues • Render modals, tooltips, dropdowns at document body level Key behavior: • DOM placement changes, but React tree stays the same • Events still bubble through the React tree (not DOM tree!) • Context still works across the portal boundary When to use: • Modals and dialogs • Tooltips and popovers • Toast notifications • Anything that needs to visually 'break out' of its container

cards.front: What are the key new features in React 19? (use, actions, form actions)
How do you use React Profiler and other tools to debug performance?
cards.back:
Built-in tools:

• <Profiler> component — measures render cost:
```jsx
<Profiler id="sidebar" onRender={(id, phase, duration) => {
  console.log(`${id} ${phase}: ${duration}ms`);
}}>
  <Sidebar />
</Profiler>
```

• React DevTools Profiler tab:
  - Record renders, see flame graph
  - 'Why did this render?' toggle
  - Highlight components that re-render

• React DevTools Settings:
  - Enable 'Highlight updates' for visual re-render detection

Debugging checklist:
• Wrap expensive children in React.memo()
• Memoize callbacks with useCallback, objects with useMemo
• Check if context providers cause waterfall re-renders
• Look for new object/array literals in JSX props
• Use useTransition for non-urgent updates

External tools:
• why-did-you-render library — logs unnecessary re-renders
• Chrome Performance tab — trace long tasks
• React Scan — visual re-render overlay
